caveats: a: The sources characterize the broader wood-manufacturing and production workforce rather than measuring AI task exposure in this six-digit industry.
a: The frozen labor ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and applies a cross-code harmonization divisor.
rho: No representative five-year implementation study was found for NAICS 321992.
rho: Benefits requiring major robotics investment are outside the exposed software-task pool unless AI is the enabling layer.
e: The 160-firm dataset input is a margin-bridged estimate rather than an observed qualifying-firm census.
e: Repeat product purchases may still be transactional and therefore ineligible.
s5: Neither source isolates prefabricated wood buildings or the $1-10M EBITDA band.
s5: Internal family succession and closures must be separated from qualifying control transfers.
q: No public pricing-contract sample for qualifying private firms was found.
q: Retention varies sharply between engineered custom buildings and standardized commodity structures.
d5: Broad construction and manufactured-housing sources do not isolate prefabricated wood buildings.
d5: The Census Manufactured Housing Survey explicitly excludes modular homes, so it is useful for boundary definition but not a direct volume series for this code.
o: Operator persistence is a judgment based on the physical production process, not a directly observed five-year share.
o: The range does not assume that today's independent supplier ownership structure is permanent.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Products are normally priced per building, kit, model, or project; the qualifying subset earns repeat revenue from customers that regularly outsource off-site fabrication.
lens.deviation_from_default: Because the code primarily manufactures products, the lens is narrowed to ongoing B2B outsourced-fabrication relationships; transactional one-off sellers are excluded for coherence.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off consumer shed or cabin sales, general contractors, on-site installers, captive component plants, retailers, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market makers of prefabricated wood buildings and building components whose builder, dealer, developer, institutional, or commercial customers repeatedly outsource fabrication.

a.bridge: BLS describes wood-product work as sawing, planing, shaping, laminating, and assembling, while production occupations operate machinery and equipment to assemble goods. I translate that embodied work mix into limited AI exposure, with exposure concentrated in design assistance, estimating, takeoffs, procurement, production planning, customer service, and documentation. Broad sector evidence may understate digital work at engineered-prefabrication firms and overstate it at simple shed plants.
caveats: The sources characterize the broader wood-manufacturing and production workforce rather than measuring AI task exposure in this six-digit industry.
The frozen labor ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and applies a cross-code harmonization divisor.
a.change_evidence: A 321992-specific wage-weighted OEWS occupation table plus task-time studies across modular-building, panelized-building, and small-structure plants.
a.rationale: Factory cutting, framing, assembly, finishing, and handling dominate the physical workflow. AI can materially assist pre-production information work and coordination, but software cannot directly substitute most shop-floor hours without complementary automation.

caveats: Broad construction and manufactured-housing sources do not isolate prefabricated wood buildings.
The Census Manufactured Housing Survey explicitly excludes modular homes, so it is useful for boundary definition but not a direct volume series for this code.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-specification order and backlog data from the Modular Building Institute or a representative manufacturer panel, split by residential, commercial, institutional, and small-structure end markets.
d5.rationale: Off-site wood construction can benefit from housing needs, labor scarcity, factory consistency, and shorter site schedules, but volumes remain exposed to rates, commercial and residential construction cycles, lumber prices, codes, permitting, and local acceptance. A modest growth base with a wide range is appropriate.

caveats: The 160-firm dataset input is a margin-bridged estimate rather than an observed qualifying-firm census.
Repeat product purchases may still be transactional and therefore ineligible.
e.change_evidence: Customer concentration and invoice-frequency data for the dataset-estimated 160 LMM firms, mapped to dealer, builder, developer, institutional, and one-off consumer channels.
e.rationale: The code mixes one-off product sellers with B2B fabricators serving repeat builders, dealers, developers, and institutions. Only the latter relationships fit the recurring outsourced-service lens, but they are likely more common here than in direct retail manufactured-home sales.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 321992 ($1,077,169,809) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 321992 ($7,885,888,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $333,752 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 321992); class <5: 259 firms, avg revenue/firm $799,873 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $117,181 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 111 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,157,117 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $316,018 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 109 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,511,037 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $660,867 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,011,992 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,906,257 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,560,725 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,260,646 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $200,218,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,331,971 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 160

caveats: Operator persistence is a judgment based on the physical production process, not a directly observed five-year share.
The range does not assume that today's independent supplier ownership structure is permanent.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy surveys, vertical-integration announcements, plant-consolidation data, and evidence on highly automated factories displacing independent fabricators.
o.rationale: Buildings and components still require accountable engineering translation, material procurement, fabrication, quality control, delivery coordination, and warranty support. AI and self-service design may compress commercial and engineering labor, but software cannot supply the physical structure; some volume could move to integrated builders or automated mega-plants.

caveats: No public pricing-contract sample for qualifying private firms was found.
Retention varies sharply between engineered custom buildings and standardized commodity structures.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract and gross-margin cohort data showing fixed-price duration, escalation clauses, bid frequency, renewal repricing, and customer concentration.
q.rationale: Quoted project and model pricing can preserve early efficiency benefits, especially where engineering, lead time, or customization differentiates the supplier. Repeat builders and dealers can re-bid programs and demand lower prices, while commodity kits face faster competitive pass-through.

caveats: No representative five-year implementation study was found for NAICS 321992.
Benefits requiring major robotics investment are outside the exposed software-task pool unless AI is the enabling layer.
rho.change_evidence: Representative adoption and ROI data for AI-assisted BIM/CAD, estimating, ERP planning, purchasing, and visual quality control at prefabrication plants.
rho.rationale: Estimating, CAD assistance, bills of materials, purchasing, scheduling, work instructions, and customer communications are plausible five-year deployments. Fragmented software, custom designs, code compliance, integration with CNC equipment, and costly production errors constrain realization.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year ownership-transfer plans across US employer businesses, and BizBuySell records small manufacturing transactions. I use those populations as a transfer prior, with a modest allowance for private founder ownership but a discount for plant specificity, real estate, working capital, and the larger EBITDA lens. Broker-marketplace data skew smaller and more readily sold, so they likely overstate target-firm liquidity.
caveats: Neither source isolates prefabricated wood buildings or the $1-10M EBITDA band.
Internal family succession and closures must be separated from qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year control-transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, and industry brokers matched to an owner-age and succession survey of 321992 firms.
s5.rationale: Private manufacturing businesses do change hands and aging owners create succession supply. Qualifying transfers nevertheless require transferable customer relationships, capable management, financeable equipment and facilities, and clean environmental and product-liability histories.

principal_driver: Digitizable design-to-production coordination layered onto a durable need for faster, more consistent off-site construction.
principal_weakness: Most value creation still depends on physical plant execution, while only a minority of firms clearly fit the recurring-service lens.
